title: Amazon EKS Auto Mode | Fully Automated Kubernetes Clusters
summary: Amazon EKS Auto Mode is a fully managed Kubernetes service that provisions production-grade clusters with a single click. It automates both control plane and data plane management while allowing customization for power users, and includes built-in features like Karpenter-based auto-scaling and cost optimization for AI workloads.
writeup: An AWS engineer walks through EKS Auto Mode, the managed Kubernetes offering that handles both control plane and data plane so teams can deploy production-grade clusters with a single API call or console click. The conversation covers the full stack from auto scaling (built on the open-source Karpenter project) and cost optimization to the specific demands of AI inference and training workloads, where more than 80% of production AI runs on Kubernetes. The through-line is a deliberate design choice: strong defaults that work out of the box, fully customizable through standard Kubernetes APIs when teams need more control.
key_points: EKS Auto Mode manages both the Kubernetes control plane and the data plane (the EC2 instances and in-cluster software where applications actually run), reducing cluster setup to a single API call or console click that yields a production-ready cluster.

Auto scaling in Auto Mode is built on the open-source Karpenter project, using pre-configured node pools that can be customized with standard Kubernetes CRDs to select instance types, enable Spot or Graviton or GPU instances, and tune cost-optimization policies.

Cost optimization runs continuously by default: when a new workload arrives and capacity is needed, Auto Mode selects the most cost-effective EC2 instance that just meets the CPU and memory requirements, while background consolidation constantly evaluates whether the existing compute can be reconfigured more cheaply.

Over 80% of AI workloads run on Kubernetes, and Auto Mode addresses their distinct requirements by splitting them into two categories: inference (bursty, unpredictable traffic requiring reactive fast scale-up of expensive GPU instances) and training (a separate resource pattern with different scheduling needs).

For inference workloads specifically, fast scale-up speed matters because GPU instances cost dollars per hour rather than cents, so minimizing the time between a traffic burst and the moment new capacity is actually serving requests directly controls cost and latency.

Customers who need deeper control can override Auto Mode defaults through standard Kubernetes APIs, including custom resources for instance selection, storage configuration for stateful workloads, and custom node pools, so managed convenience does not require surrendering configurability.
